hypotheses: Mortality salience will produce divergent cultural responses.
European Americans will use self-protective strategies after mortality salience.
Asian Americans will affirm others instead of the self after mortality salience.
claim.statement: The main claim made by the original study that matches the claim given as an input.

data.notes: The PANAS was used as a delay task.

method.description: Two experiments were conducted to compare the effects of mortality salience on cultural norms.
steps: Participants were primed with either death or a control condition.
Attitudes towards culturally normative and non-normative individuals were measured.
Study 1 focused on attitudes towards a prostitute, and Study 2 on an innocent victim.
method.outcome_variable: Attitudes towards a dissimilar other and assignment of blame

results.summary: Mortality salience led to culturally divergent responses—a defensive stance in European Americans and a more affirming stance in Asian Americans.

metadata.original_paper_title: Culturally Divergent Responses to Mortality Salience
